Eight months in, it is worth taking stock of what has actually moved and what has not.
We fell short of the response rate I projected in June, and I would rather report that than bury it.
The focus groups worked. What I propose is to build on them rather than start again.
The framework is sound. We have six weeks to iron out the details before the launch.
On whether the new onboarding is working, the jury is still out. Ask me again in March.
Having reviewed the findings, I would propose that we build on the focus groups.
The trust index came back at fifty-eight, leaving us nine points below the benchmark.
Asked about the timeline, most of the faculty said exactly the same thing.
Given the response rate, the caveat belongs on the first slide, not the last.
You have eleven minutes and I have read nothing. Start wherever you like, but I will tell you now that I stop listening around slide three.

Then I will give you the takeaway before the first slide. The culture here is not broken. It is unheard, and the response rate is the evidence. Everything I say in the next ten minutes defends that one sentence.
Good. Then give me a number, and give me the one you would rather I did not see.

Thirty-eight percent. I promised you seventy in June, so we fell short of my own target, and that is my caveat: the sample is smaller than I wanted. Given what the focus groups said, though, I would argue the thirty-eight is itself the finding. A faculty that was surveyed once and never told the outcome does not answer twice.
So what do you want from me? And do not tell me you want more time. Everyone wants more time.

One decision, today. Having run four focus groups, I would build on them rather than start again: two quick wins before December, and the full framework from Q2. If you want the framework pulled forward, you can have it, and here is the trade-off: the baseline gets taken after the change instead of before, and we lose the ability to prove any of it worked.
Having taken stock of the first six months, I would propose two quick wins and one long piece of work.
We fell short of the response rate we projected, and I would rather report that than bury it.
Given the preliminary numbers, one caveat belongs on the first slide, not the last.
The focus groups worked, so what I propose is to build on them rather than start again.

Danielle, it is Claire. I have your draft deck for the midpoint review in front of me, and I want to change three things before Warren sees it. The first one is slide one. You open with the methodology and the four phases of the assessment, and Warren will stop reading before he reaches anything you found. Put the takeaway on slide one instead. One sentence, before any detail, before any number. The culture here is not broken, it is unheard. That sentence. The second thing is the thirty-eight percent. It is on slide fourteen, and I understand why you put it there, but you have to say it yourself, early, and you have to call it a caveat. A number you announce is a finding. The same number, discovered by somebody else halfway through your deck, is a cover-up, and it will cost you every other number in the review. And the third thing, Danielle. Do not promise him that the new onboarding is working. It might be. The first cohort has not finished, and you do not know yet. Say that the jury is still out, give him a date, and tell him you will have a tangible number in March. He will respect that far more than he would respect a yes that you cannot defend in the spring.

Danielle, Warren here. Claire tells me you are presenting the midpoint tomorrow, so let me save you some time and tell you how I listen. With most reviews, I stop somewhere around slide three. Not because I am rude, but because by slide three I still do not know what the person found. I only know how they went about finding it, and that is their work, not my decision. So here is what I actually want from your eleven minutes. What you found, in one sentence. What it is going to cost me. And what you want me to decide, today, in that room. Anything else is you thinking out loud, and you can do that with Claire. One more thing, and I mean this. If you bring me only good news, I will not believe you. In twenty-two years I have never seen a project at the halfway mark where everything worked, so a leader who tells me that has either not measured it, or has measured it and is not telling me. Bring me the number you fell short of. I would very much rather hear the caveat from you tomorrow than find it for myself in March.
